name: readability
description: Analyze text readability with Flesch-Kincaid, Gunning Fog, SMOG, and other metrics. Returns objective scores with interpretation and recommendations.
capabilities: Calculate Flesch Reading Ease score (0-100 scale)
Compute Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level for US education standards
Generate Gunning Fog Index to measure years of education needed
Determine SMOG Index for grade-level estimation
Analyze text statistics including word count, sentence length, and complex word percentage
Provide specific recommendations to improve readability based on scores
author: humanizerai
securitySummary: All static analysis findings are false positives. The skill contains only markdown documentation with no executable code. The flagged 'external_commands' pattern (line 40) is within a code block demonstrating output format, not executable Ruby/shell code. The 'blocker' patterns matched common English words in documentation. This skill performs pure text analysis with no security risks.
valueStatement: Struggle to gauge if your writing matches your audience's reading level? This skill calculates industry-standard readability metrics (Flesch-Kincaid, Gunning Fog, SMOG) and provides specific recommendations to simplify complex text.
limitations: Requires text input - cannot analyze files or URLs directly
Relies on syllable counting which may be inaccurate for technical terms or proper nouns
Passive voice detection is estimated, not grammatically precise
Designed for English text - metrics may not apply to other languages

useCases.title: Simplify Technical Documentation
useCases.description: Transform complex API documentation or technical manuals into content accessible to non-technical users by identifying grade level and complex word density.

useCases.title: Optimize Marketing Copy
useCases.description: Ensure blog posts, landing pages, and email campaigns resonate with target audiences by matching readability to their education level.

useCases.title: Improve Academic Communication
useCases.description: Adjust research paper abstracts or grant proposals to meet specific reading level requirements for journals or funding agencies.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Readability Check
promptTemplates.prompt: Analyze the readability of this text: [paste your text here]
promptTemplates.scenario: Quick check for any text to get overall readability scores

promptTemplates.title: Compare Two Versions
promptTemplates.prompt:
Compare the readability of these two texts and explain which is more accessible:

Version A: [text]

Version B: [text]
promptTemplates.scenario: A/B testing different writing styles or revisions

promptTemplates.title: Grade Level Target
promptTemplates.prompt:
Analyze this text and tell me if it's appropriate for 8th grade reading level. If not, what should I change?

[paste text]
promptTemplates.scenario: Writing for specific education levels or audiences

promptTemplates.title: Comprehensive Analysis with Fixes
promptTemplates.prompt:
Analyze my text for readability and provide 5 specific sentence-level improvements I can make right now:

[paste your text]
promptTemplates.scenario: Detailed feedback with actionable revision suggestions

bestPractices: Provide at least 3-4 sentences (50-100 words minimum) for accurate metrics - shorter samples may produce unreliable scores
Use readability scores as one data point alongside audience analysis and domain knowledge - technical audiences may expect higher complexity
Run analysis on complete paragraphs rather than isolated sentences to capture natural sentence variety
Combine readability scores with user testing when possible - metrics measure complexity, not comprehension
antiPatterns: Do not chase lower grade levels indiscriminately - some topics require precision and complexity regardless of scores
Avoid simplifying text at the cost of accuracy - technical terminology exists for valid reasons
Do not use readability scores as the sole quality metric - context, purpose, and audience matter more than numerical grades
Never ignore domain-specific vocabulary - forcing simplification of precise terms can confuse expert readers

faq.question: What is a good readability score?
faq.answer: It depends on your audience. Aim for 60-70 (8th-9th grade) for general public, 80+ (6th grade) for children, and 30-50 (college level) for academic or professional audiences.

faq.question: Why do the different metrics give different grade levels?
faq.answer: Each formula emphasizes different aspects. Flesch-Kincaid focuses on sentence and word length. Gunning Fog counts complex words. SMOG emphasizes polysyllabic words. They measure complexity differently.

faq.question: Can this tool check grammar or spelling?
faq.answer: No. This skill only calculates readability metrics and provides style recommendations. Use dedicated grammar tools for spelling and grammar checking.

faq.question: How accurate is the syllable counting?
faq.answer: Syllable counting is estimated and may be inaccurate for technical terms, proper nouns, or non-English words. Treat scores as approximations, not exact measurements.

faq.question: Does this work for languages other than English?
faq.answer: No. These metrics are designed specifically for English text. Other languages require different readability formulas and syllabification rules.

faq.question: What text length provides the most accurate results?
faq.answer: At least 100 words (3-5 paragraphs) provides reliable scores. Shorter samples under 50 words may produce skewed results due to limited sample size.
